mathematical_operation: Define the two-point response kernel R_ij(k) as a rank-2 symmetric tensor in d-dimensional momentum space.
human_readable_reason: Establish the primary object of the derivation before any manipulation.
assumptions: R_ij = R_ji (symmetry)
Existence of a well-defined momentum-space representation

mathematical_operation: Define sector A kernel A_ij(k) = f(k) delta_ij + g(k) k_i k_j / k^2 and sector B kernel B_ij(k) = h(k) (delta_ij - k_i k_j / k^2).
human_readable_reason: Decompose the full kernel into physically distinct sectors before integration.
assumptions: f(k), g(k), h(k) are real and differentiable on (0, Lambda]
k^2 != 0 for k > 0
A_ij and B_ij are symmetric rank-2 tensors

index_scope.summation_conventions: Einstein summation over repeated tensor indices

symmetry_properties.B_ij: symmetric and transverse: k_i B_ij = 0, B_ij = B_ji

mathematical_operation: Write the defining integral R_ij = integral_0^Lambda dk/k [A_ij(k) + B_ij(k)] + O(epsilon^2).
human_readable_reason: Express the response kernel as a radial momentum integral of the sector-summed kernel, establishing the starting point for all subsequent manipulations.
assumptions: Integral converges on (0, Lambda]
O(epsilon^2) bound accounts for all corrections beyond linear order in epsilon
The measure dk/k is the natural radial measure in d = 2 + epsilon

index_scope.summation_conventions: Einstein summation over repeated tensor indices only; k is an integration variable, not a dummy index

boundary_conditions: Integration limits: k in [0, Lambda] with cutoff regularization
caveats: O(epsilon^2) error estimate requires regularity assumptions on f, g, h

mathematical_operation: Apply the product rule identity partial_k [F(k) G_ij(k)] = F'(k) G_ij(k) + F(k) partial_k G_ij(k) to reorganize the integrand structure.
human_readable_reason: The product rule allows reorganization of the integrand into total derivatives (boundary terms) plus remainder terms, facilitating subsequent integration.
assumptions: F(k) is differentiable on (0, Lambda]
G_ij(k) is differentiable on (0, Lambda] for all i, j
Partial_k commutes with tensor index operations in flat Euclidean space

index_scope.summation_conventions: Einstein summation over repeated tensor indices

mathematical_operation: Reorganize the integrand by applying the product rule, separating total derivative terms partial_k[...] from remainder algebraic terms.
human_readable_reason: Separation of total derivatives from remainder terms exposes the structure that will simplify under integration and projection.
assumptions: The product rule applies term-by-term to each contribution in A_ij and B_ij
Total derivative terms integrate to boundary values at k = 0 and k = Lambda
No integration by parts is performed at this stage — only algebraic reorganization

index_domains.l: contracted dummy index in tensor products

index_scope.summation_conventions: Einstein summation over repeated indices l

symmetry_properties.reorganized_expression: preserves symmetry of R_ij under i <-> j

boundary_conditions: Boundary terms to be evaluated at integration stage (step_integ_A, step_integ_B)

mathematical_operation: Decompose the reorganized expression into sector A (carrying f(k) and g(k)) and sector B (carrying h(k)), verifying exact reconstruction R_ij = R_ij^(A) + R_ij^(B).
human_readable_reason: Physical sectors correspond to different tensor structures (scalar+longitudinal vs. transverse) and may have distinct physical origins.
assumptions: A_ij and B_ij are linearly independent as tensor-valued functions of k
The decomposition is exact: no terms involve products of A and B kernels at the same k
Each sector inherits the symmetry properties of its kernel

mathematical_operation: Project the sector-decomposed expressions onto the basis tensors delta_ij and I_ij to obtain scalar coefficient equations for C_0 and C_2.
human_readable_reason: Tensor projection reduces the problem from tensor equations to scalar equations, isolating the two independent physical parameters.
assumptions: {delta_ij, I_ij} spans the space of symmetric rank-2 tensors with a single preferred direction n in d dimensions
Contractions: delta_ij delta_ij = d, I_ij I_ij = 1, delta_ij I_ij = 0 (tracelessness)
Projection is orthogonal: A_ij B_ij = 0 for the two basis tensors

index_scope.summation_conventions: Einstein summation over all repeated indices

projection_scope: Contraction with delta_ij yields trace (C_0); contraction with I_ij yields anisotropic coefficient (C_2)
caveats: Projection assumes completeness of the 2-tensor basis, which holds only in the presence of a single preferred direction

mathematical_operation: Integrate the projected sector A contribution over the radial momentum coordinate k from 0 to Lambda, evaluating boundary terms at both limits.
human_readable_reason: The radial integration produces the cutoff-dependent coefficients C_0 and C_2 that characterize the physical response.
assumptions: Boundary terms at k = 0 vanish: lim_{k->0} k [f(k), g(k)] = 0 (infrared regularity)
Boundary terms at k = Lambda are finite and contribute to C_0, C_2
Integration commutes with tensor projection and contraction

index_domains.k: radial momentum, real positive in (0, Lambda]

projection_scope: Radial integration after projection onto scalar coefficients
boundary_conditions: lim_{k->0} k f(k) = 0, lim_{k->0} k g(k) = 0 (infrared); finite at k = Lambda
caveats: Exact analytic form of C_0 and C_2 depends on the specific functions f(k), g(k)

mathematical_operation: Integrate the projected sector B (transverse) contribution over k from 0 to Lambda.
human_readable_reason: The transverse sector integration completes the determination of the two physical coefficients C_0 and C_2.
assumptions: Boundary terms at k = 0 vanish: lim_{k->0} k h(k) = 0 (infrared regularity)
Transverse projector property k_i B_ij = 0 simplifies angular integration in d dimensions
The angular integral of the transverse projector yields factors of (d-1)/d

index_domains.k: radial momentum, real positive in (0, Lambda]

projection_scope: Integration after projection onto scalar coefficients and use of transverse projector angular average
boundary_conditions: lim_{k->0} k h(k) = 0 (infrared); finite at k = Lambda
caveats: The angular integration factor (d-1)/d is exact in d dimensions for the transverse projector

mathematical_operation: Combine the integrated sector A and sector B contributions to obtain the final compact form R_ij = C_0 delta_ij + C_2 I_ij + O(epsilon^2, 1/Lambda).
human_readable_reason: The combination yields the simplified two-parameter form that is the primary result of the derivation.
assumptions: Sector A and sector B contributions add linearly (no cross-interference beyond what is captured in the kernel decomposition)
Error terms O(epsilon^2, 1/Lambda) account for truncation and finite-cutoff corrections
The basis {delta_ij, I_ij} is complete for the final tensor structure

index_scope.summation_conventions: Einstein summation not applicable (no contractions in final form)

boundary_conditions: C_0 and C_2 encode all boundary and cutoff dependence
caveats: Error term O(epsilon^2, 1/Lambda) is a bound, not an exact equality

mathematical_operation: Take the limit epsilon -> 0+ in the final expression, showing that I_ij = 0 in d = 2 and thus R_ij -> C_0^(0) delta_ij.
human_readable_reason: The dimensional reduction limit provides a nontrivial consistency check and reveals the special nature of d = 2 where the anisotropic response vanishes identically.
assumptions: The limit epsilon -> 0+ commutes with the radial integration (uniform convergence)
C_0^(0) = lim_{epsilon->0} C_0 is finite
In d = 2, the identity delta_ij = 2 n_i n_j / n^2 holds for the unique unit vector n, forcing I_ij = 0
boundary_conditions: C_0^(0) is the finite limit of the isotropic coefficient
caveats: The vanishing of I_ij relies on algebraic identities specific to d = 2

mathematical_operation: Perform numerical regression: evaluate both the 96-term expanded expression and the compact 2-term result for sample values of f(k), g(k), h(k), epsilon, and Lambda, confirming agreement within O(epsilon^2, 1/Lambda).
human_readable_reason: Numerical verification provides independent support for the correctness of the algebraic simplification, catching potential errors in tensor contractions or sign conventions.
assumptions: Sample functions f(k), g(k), h(k) are representative and probe the full tensor structure
Numerical integration error is controlled and smaller than the expected O(epsilon^2, 1/Lambda) difference
The 96-term expression is the complete expansion of the starting expression

index_scope.summation_conventions: Einstein summation over repeated tensor indices

projection_scope: Component-wise comparison of all d^2 components of R_ij
boundary_conditions: Numerical integration uses the same cutoff Lambda and boundary conditions as the analytic derivation
caveats: Numerical verification supports but does not prove the analytic result
The sample parameter space is finite; pathological counterexamples could exist outside the tested region

mathematical_operation: Provide physical interpretation: C_0 controls isotropic response, C_2 controls anisotropic response; sector A encodes compressible modes, sector B encodes incompressible modes.
human_readable_reason: The mathematical results must be connected to physical observables to be useful for experimental predictions and theoretical understanding.
assumptions: Linear response theory provides the framework for interpreting C_0 and C_2 as transport coefficients
The preferred direction n has a physical origin (external field, boundary, or spontaneous symmetry breaking)
Observable signatures are accessible via directional or angular-resolved measurements
caveats: Physical interpretation is subjective and model-dependent
